topics: 🏺 Two potters in single file: when a fall makes the first liable for the second
🚶 Right of way — beam vs. amphora vs. donkeys vs. ships
⏸️ R' Yochanan's question: do we give the fallen one time to stand?
💥 R' Ze'ira: what if they shifted course (שִׁנּוּ) and damaged?
🚪 Who yields — entering vs. leaving the bathhouse and toilet

overview.title: 📖 Overview — Collisions in the Public Domain
overview.label: The sugya:
overview.text: The Mishnah opens a new sub-topic: damage between two people both legitimately walking in רשות הרבים. The Yerushalmi pushes on the timing edges (does the first get a moment to stand?), extends the case to three, four, even a hundred, and then maps a hierarchy of right-of-way rules — loaded vs. empty, ships vs. carts, even bathhouse vs. toilet etiquette.
overview.hebrewSource: שְׁנֵי קַדָּרִין שֶׁהָיוּ מְהַלְּכִין זֶה אַחַר זֶה וְנִתְקַל הָרִאשׁוֹן וְנָפַל וְנִתְקַל הַשֵּׁנִי בָּרִאשׁוֹן הָרִאשׁוֹן חַיָיב בְּנִזְקֵי הַשֵּׁנִי

slides.title: 🏺 Two Potters Walking in File
slides.subtitle: The base case the Mishnah opens with.

boxes.title: 1️⃣ First Stumbles
items: Becomes a hazard in the path
Effectively a בור the second can't avoid

boxes.title: 2️⃣ Second Falls Over Him
items: His pots break on the first one's body

boxes.title: 🚧 Why Liable?
items: Once down, he should warn or move
The Yerushalmi treats him like a stationary obstacle
Compare 16a: standing in the road = קֶרֶן זָוִית

callout.label: Key move:
callout.text: The first potter is no longer just a walker. After he falls, he becomes a hazard — and ownership of the hazard means liability for what trips on it.

slides.title: 💬 R' Yochanan's Question — A Moment to Stand?
slides.subtitle: How long after the fall does liability attach?

lines.speaker: RY
lines.text: מַהוּ שֶׁיִּתֵּן לָרִאשׁוֹן שָׁהוּת כְּדֵי עֲמִידָה? — Do we give the first potter time to get up before holding him liable?

lines.speaker: R' Yose
lines.text: פְּשִׁיטָא לְרִבִּי יוֹחָנָן לִיתֵּן לָרִאשׁוֹן שָׁהוּת — for R' Yochanan it is obvious we grant him time to stand.

lines.speaker: Y
lines.text: But what about the next one in line — does he get the same grace period? Probably not: from the second onwards, you saw the chain reaction and should know to stop.

lines.speaker: Beraita
lines.text: Three, four, a hundred fall in a chain — first pays second, second pays third. וְאִם מַחֲמַת הָרִאשׁוֹן נָפְלוּ כוּלָּן הוּא מְשַׁלֵּם עַל יְדֵי כוּלָּן.

callout.label: Important caveat:
callout.text: Liability is for damage to people; for damage to vessels in the chain, all are exempt — the Torah only obligates an animal/person who actively damaged, not a passive victim turned obstacle.

slides.title: 🛣️ Right-of-Way Hierarchy
slides.subtitle: Who yields to whom — the Yerushalmi's practical road code.
rows: Loaded one is harder to maneuver
Same logic — protect the encumbered party
Hot inside; let the wet/hot one out first
Privacy and urgency of the one going in

callout.label: Yerushalmi style:
callout.text: The sugya doesn't just decide damages — it scripts the etiquette that prevents damages. Halakhic ergonomics for the public domain.

slides.title: 💥 R' Ze'ira — When They Veered (שִׁנּוּ)
slides.subtitle: The amphora vs. beam case opens an edge question.

steps.content: 1️⃣ Mishnah: one walks with a jug, one with a beam — neither liable, both have רְשׁוּת לְהַלֵּךְ.
2️⃣ R' Ze'ira asks: שִׁנּוּ וְהִזִּיקוּ מַהוּ? — what if one veered out of his lane and damaged?
3️⃣ Compare: five sat on a bench; a sixth sat down and it broke.
4️⃣ The sixth alone pays — he is the one who changed the equilibrium.
5️⃣ But if it broke מֵחֲמַת כּוּלָּן, all are liable together.

callout.label: Bottom line:
callout.text: Permission to walk does not include permission to deviate. Once you change your trajectory and that change causes damage, the exemption of רְשׁוּת לְהַלֵּךְ evaporates.

slides.title: 🏗️ Beam-Carrier vs. Jug-Carrier (3:6:1)
slides.subtitle: The decisive variable: who is in front, and did anyone stop?

nodes.content:
Beam first, jug last
Jug breaks on beam
Same logic flips when the jug-carrier is in front — the front one bears liability if he stops without warning.
וְכֵן זֶה בָּא בְנֵירוֹ וְזֶה בָּא בְפִשְׁתָּנוֹ — same rules for fire-bearer and flax-bearer.

callout.label: The principle:
callout.text: Walking is licensed; stopping silently is not. A sudden halt converts a walker into a hazard, and warning shifts the responsibility to the one behind.

summary.principle: Walkers in the public domain are mutually exempt — until one falls, stops, or veers. The moment someone becomes a static hazard or changes course, liability snaps onto him.

flowSteps.content:
Two people walking, one falls or stops
נִתְקַל הָרִאשׁוֹן וְנָפַל
Did the fallen/stopped person have time to recover or warn?

🎓 Key Principle:
The right to walk is not a blanket immunity. Liability tracks who became the hazard — and whether warning was given.

quiz.q: In the opening Mishnah of two potters, why is the first liable for the second's damages?
o: Because he was walking faster
Because once he fell, he became a hazard the second couldn't avoid
Because the public domain belongs to him
Because his pot was bigger
quiz.e: נִתְקַל הָרִאשׁוֹן וְנָפַל — once down, he is effectively a stationary obstacle, like a בור in the road.

quiz.q: What was R' Yochanan's specific question about the fallen first potter?
o: Whether he must compensate for his own broken jug
מַהוּ שֶׁיִּתֵּן לָרִאשׁוֹן שָׁהוּת כְּדֵי עֲמִידָה — do we give him time to stand before liability attaches?
Whether the second potter must pay him for embarrassment
Whether women can also be liable in this case
quiz.e: R' Yose answers that for R' Yochanan it is obvious — yes, the first gets a grace period.

quiz.q: When two animals meet on the road, one loaded and one empty, who has the right of way?
o: The empty one
Whoever is faster
The loaded one
Whoever started walking first
quiz.e: מַעֲבִירִין אֶת הָרֵיקָן מִפְּנֵי הַטָּעוּן — the loaded one has priority since maneuvering it is harder.

quiz.q: In the Mishnah of beam-carrier and jug-carrier, when is the beam-carrier (in front) liable?
o: Always
Never — both have permission to walk
Only if he stopped walking without warning
Only if the jug-carrier asked him to stop
quiz.e: וְאִם עָמַד בַּעַל הַקּוֹרָה חַיָיב — but if he warned the jug-carrier to stop, he is exempt.

quiz.q: What does the bench example (5+1 sitters) teach about R' Ze'ira's question?
o: Damages are split equally always
If the bench breaks because of the new arrival alone, only he pays; if all caused it, all pay
Sitting is never liable
Wood vessels are exempt
quiz.e: הוּא מְשַׁלֵּם עַל יְדֵי כוּלָּן. וְאִם מִחְמַת כּוּלָּן נִשְׁבַּר כּוּלָּן חַיָיבִין — liability follows the cause.
